does: This paper provides a comprehensive survey of mechanistic interpretability research applied to transformer language models, organizing the field through a task-centric taxonomy. The authors systematically review the fundamental objects of study in MI (such as attention heads, neurons, and circuits), catalog the main techniques used to analyze these components, and synthesize key findings from recent work. The paper identifies the primary research questions driving MI investigations and structures findings around specific interpretability tasks, establishing evaluation methods and discovered insights for each category. Rather than advancing a novel technique, this work synthesizes existing knowledge to create a structured roadmap of the field's current state, covering both the conceptual foundations and practical tools used to reverse-engineer transformer computations.
connects: This review builds on the growing body of mechanistic interpretability work that emerged as a response to the black-box nature of deep neural networks. It connects to earlier interpretability research including attention visualization, feature attribution methods, and circuit analysis, while specifically focusing on recent breakthroughs in understanding transformers. The paper responds to the expansion of MI applications to language models following influential work on neural circuit discovery and attention head interpretation. It also engages with broader interpretability traditions seeking to understand neural network internals through probing tasks, ablation studies, and causal analysis. The task-centric organization reflects and extends frameworks from interpretability research that organize work around interpretable building blocks rather than monolithic explanations.
enables: This comprehensive review establishes a foundation for future MI research by identifying gaps and suggesting high-impact research directions. By providing a structured taxonomy and accessibility guide for newcomers, the work lowers barriers to entry and facilitates faster progress on unsolved interpretability challenges. The survey enables researchers to systematically identify missing pieces in our understanding of transformer computations and develop better evaluation standards for interpretability work. It supports the development of more robust interpretability techniques and could facilitate applications to AI safety and alignment, where understanding model internals is critical. The organized knowledge also enables more effective cross-pollination between MI and other subfields, potentially accelerating discovery of generalizable interpretability principles applicable beyond language models.
